title: Positive-definite symplectic four-manifolds
speaker: Jennifer Hom
abstract: Workshop on Flows, Foliations and Contact Structures 2015-2016 Monday, December 7, 2015 - 08:00 to Friday, December 11, 2015 - 12:00 This workshop is part of the topical program "Geometric Structures on 3-Manifolds" which will take place during the 2015-2016 academic year at the Institute for Advanced Study. This workshop will focus on the relations between flows, 2-dimensional foliations, and contact structures on 3-manifolds.
tags: Workshop on Flows Foliations and Contact Structures

proof_stages.label: Positive-definite symplectic four-manifold problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the geography/exotic-smoothness motivation, specializes to simply connected positive-definite four-manifolds, defines geometric simple connectivity, and states the main theorem that a closed geometrically simply connected positive-definite four-manifold with b2+ at least 2 is not symplectic.

proof_stages.label: Perfect-Morse corollary in the b2+=1 case
proof_stages.summary: Using the main theorem, a simply connected positive-definite symplectic four-manifold with a perfect Morse function must have b2+=1, hence a handle decomposition given by one +1-framed two-handle, and Gordon--Luecke's surgery result forces the attaching knot to be the unknot, giving CP2.

proof_stages.label: Heegaard Floer ingredients and symplectic nonvanishing
proof_stages.summary: The proof is set up by recalling the Heegaard Floer closed four-manifold invariant, the Ozsvath--Szabo symplectic nonvanishing theorem, the HF± TQFT cobordism maps, reduced Floer homology, and the d-invariant.

proof_stages.label: Mixed-map definition of the closed four-manifold invariant
proof_stages.summary: After removing two balls from a closed four-manifold and cutting along an admissible three-manifold with b2+ positive on both sides, the invariant is defined by a mixed HF− to HF+ map factoring through HFred.

proof_stages.label: Surgery-trace vanishing proposition
proof_stages.summary: The key technical proposition says that if d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0, then the Heegaard Floer cobordism map for the trace of positive n-surgery on K is zero in every Spin^c structure, proved using the Ozsvath--Szabo mapping cone formula.

proof_stages.label: Main contradiction from the first +1 handle
proof_stages.summary: For a geometrically simply connected positive-definite four-manifold, the no-one-handle decomposition lets one cut after the first +1-framed two-handle; positivity on both sides forces d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0, so the trace proposition kills the first cobordism map and hence the mixed invariant, contradicting symplectic nonvanishing.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic filling obstruction for surgery traces
proof_stages.summary: The same trace-map vanishing proves that if d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0, then no positive surgery trace on K can be a symplectic filling, since any such filling embeds in a closed symplectic four-manifold whose Heegaard Floer invariant would be forced to vanish.

examples.name: Blown-up K3 surface as an exotic smooth model
examples.note: The blown-up K3 surface illustrates how a symplectic structure, together with Freedman theory and Taubes' obstruction, can exhibit a manifold homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to a connected sum of complex projective planes.
examples.engagement_reason: The blown-up K3 is used concretely: the speaker gives its Betti numbers/intersection-form data and applies Freedman and Taubes to derive that it is homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to the specified connected sum.

passes.reason: It is used as an exotic smooth structure homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to the standard connected sum, refuting smooth uniqueness.
The one-point blow-up of K3 is used as an exotic smooth structure on the topological manifold #3CP2 # 20CPbar, witnessing failure of homeomorphism to imply diffeomorphism.

The blown-up K3 is used to exhibit an exotic smooth structure homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to the corresponding connected sum, while not being structurally degenerate, extremal, symmetric, or sporadic-exceptional here.
The blown-up K3 is used to exhibit a smooth manifold homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to the standard connected sum, i.e. an exotic smooth model.
An exotic smooth model witnessing homeomorphic but non-diffeomorphic smooth four-manifolds.
Blown-up K3 is used as a nonstandard smooth structure homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to #3 CP^2 # 20 overline{CP}^2, refuting smooth uniqueness for that topological type.
Used to exhibit a homeomorphic-but-not-diffeomorphic smooth structure on 3CP2#20CPbar, i.e. an exotic smooth model distinguished from the standard connected sum by symplectic obstructions.
It is used to exhibit a manifold homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to the standard connected sum, and as an exotic smoothing it is structurally exceptional.
It is used to show homeomorphism need not imply diffeomorphism for the connected sum, and as such gives an exotic smooth structure on that topological 4-manifold.
It is used to exhibit a homeomorphic-but-not-diffeomorphic connected sum, and as an exotic smooth structure it is a classification exception rather than a symmetric, extremal, or degenerate object.
The blown-up K3 is used to show a manifold homeomorphic to #3CP2#20CP2bar need not be diffeomorphic to it, giving an exotic smooth structure.
The blown-up K3 is used as an exotic smooth structure homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to the standard connected sum, so it refutes smooth uniqueness for that topological type.

examples.name: Knot classes with vanishing +1-surgery correction term
examples.note: Sliced knots, negative torus knots, knots with negative L-space surgery, and alternating knots of positive signature are listed to make concrete the hypothesis d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0 in the surgery-trace proposition and filling obstruction.
examples.engagement_reason: After listing knot classes with d(+1 surgery)=0, the speaker uses a general knot with this property in the surgery-trace proposition and theorem, sketching via the mapping cone why the cobordism map vanishes and deriving the non-filling consequence.

passes.reason: These knots have the maximal possible vanishing +1-surgery correction term and are used to obstruct the surgery trace from being a symplectic filling.
These infinite knot families saturate the bound d(S^3_{+1}(K))<=0 by having d=0 and are used to give surgery traces obstructed from being symplectic fillings.
Knots with d(+1 surgery)=0 attain the maximal possible +1-surgery correction term and are used to obstruct the surgery trace from being a symplectic filling.
Knots with d(+1 surgery)=0 attain the maximal possible +1-surgery correction term and instantiate the obstruction showing their surgery traces cannot be symplectic fillings.
Broad knot families singled out by a vanishing correction term and used to obstruct the surgery trace from being a symplectic filling.
Knots with d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0 attain the maximal possible +1-surgery correction term and are used to obstruct the surgery trace from being a symplectic filling.
Knots with d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0 are used to obstruct the surgery trace from being a symplectic filling, and the vanishing value is the maximal/tight +1-surgery correction term condition.
These knots are used to obstruct the surgery trace from being a symplectic filling, and d(S^3_1(K))=0 is the boundary/maximal correction-term case.
Knots with d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0 attain the maximal possible +1-surgery correction term and are used to obstruct their surgery traces from being symplectic fillings.
These knots supply obstruction cases to symplectic filling of surgery traces, and d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0 is the boundary/maximal correction-term case in a systematic family.
Knots with d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0 attain the maximal/threshold +1-surgery correction term and are used to obstruct the surgery trace from being a symplectic filling.
Knots with d(S^3_{+1}(K))=0 are used as obstruction cases whose surgery traces cannot symplectically fill, and the vanishing d-invariant is the boundary/maximal value of that correction term.
